ner: Grantham Institute at Imperial College London
summary: In December 2015, 196 nations signed the Paris Agreement to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.
As of 2024, global temperatures are 1.45°C higher than the 1850–1900 baseline, per the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report.
Global CO₂ emissions hit a record 37.4 gigatons in 2023, with fossil fuel consumption near historic levels despite renewable energy expansion.
The European Union has reduced emissions by 32% since 1990 but Brazil faces criticism for Amazon deforestation under President Lula da Silva's administration.
India, now the third-largest emitter, plans to achieve net zero by 2070, a timeline criticized as too late by climate advocates.
Major oil and gas companies spent $750 million on lobbying against climate legislation from 2020 to 2024, according to InfluenceMap.
COP30 in Belém, Brazil, scheduled for November 2025, will assess nations' progress and commitment to the Paris Agreement.
Despite challenges, renewable energy advancements offer hope with solar capacity surpassing 2 terawatts in 2024 and battery storage costs falling significantly.

ner: Grantham Institute van het Imperial College London
summary: Ten years after the Paris Agreement, global temperatures are dangerously close to the 1.5°C threshold, at 1.45°C above pre-industrial levels in 2024, according to the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report.

In 2023, global CO2 emissions reached a record high of 37.4 gigatonnes, despite growth in renewable energy; fossil fuel use remains high, with coal consumption in South and Southeast Asia offsetting other gains, as noted by the IEA.

The European Union has reduced emissions by 32% since 1990 through its European Green Deal, but Brazil faces criticism for rapid deforestation in the Amazon under President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, with 11,568 km² lost in 2024 alone.

India, the world's second-largest emitter after China and the US, has pledged to be climate neutral by 2070, a timeline criticized as too late by activists like Greta Thunberg and the Youth Climate Coalition.

A September 2025 InfluenceMap report revealed that major oil and gas companies spent $750 million on lobbying against climate legislation between 2020-2024, with Shell's Wael Sawan dismissing the findings as selective and misleading.

The upcoming COP30 in Belém, Brazil, scheduled for November 2025, will test international resolve; UN Secretary-General António Guterres has called for updated Nationally Determined Contributions with more ambitious 2035 goals.

Despite falling short of required targets, the renewable energy sector offers hope, with solar capacity surpassing 2 terawatts in 2024 and investments by Ørsted and Iberdrola reaching records; battery storage costs have dropped 89% since 2010.
